coredata.dc:title: Transforming waste into multifunctional nanomaterials: Mg-doped carbon dots from cow dung for Y(III) detection and biomedical applications

coredata.dc:description: Rare earth metal Yttrium (Y3+) plays a vital role in many electronic devices however, discarding these devices eventually contaminates the environmental sources. Herein, we have developed a Mg-doped carbon dots (M-CDs) as a fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive determination of Y3+ in water bodies. The M-CDs having maximum emission at 420 nm upon 310 nm excitation with 20 % quantum yield and which was synthesized from upcycling of agricultural waste i.e., cow dung by simple carbonization followed by hydrothermal method. M-CDs were confirmed using different analytical and characterization techniques as well as stable in different pH and ionic strength solutions. The M-CDs was highly selective towards Y3+ ions over different metal ions with significant blue shift. This fluorescent probe performs a good linear relationship between the different concentrations of Y3+ ion and fluorescence intensity (R2 = 0.99) within the range of 0.2 to 20 μg/mL with a detection limit of 0.019 µg/mL. The study indicates quenching of Y3+ was result of dynamic quenching and the Inner Filter Effect (IFE) effect. Also, the cytotoxicity of M-CDs was checked via CAM assay and significant growth in blood vascularization with healthy growth of chick embryo confirmed the M-CDs were biocompatible. The nontoxic M-CDs was employed for MCF-7 breast cancer cell imaging which gives bright blue fluoresce under UV light excitation. Further, aiming to a circular economy approach the residual carbon remaining after hydrothermal was used as reactivated carbon for the abatement of environmental pollutants. The sustainable, cost-effective and agricultural waste-derived Mg-doped CDs have potential applications in analytical, environmental, forensic as well as biomedical fields.

head.ce:title: Transforming waste into multifunctional nanomaterials: Mg-doped carbon dots from cow dung for Y(III) detection and biomedical applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rare earth metal Yttrium (Y3+) plays a vital role in many electronic devices however, discarding these devices eventually contaminates the environmental sources. Herein, we have developed a Mg-doped carbon dots (M-CDs) as a fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive determination of Y3+ in water bodies. The M-CDs having maximum emission at 420 nm upon 310 nm excitation with 20 % quantum yield and which was synthesized from upcycling of agricultural waste i.e., cow dung by simple carbonization followed by hydrothermal method. M-CDs were confirmed using different analytical and characterization techniques as well as stable in different pH and ionic strength solutions. The M-CDs was highly selective towards Y3+ ions over different metal ions with significant blue shift. This fluorescent probe performs a good linear relationship between the different concentrations of Y3+ ion and fluorescence intensity (R2 = 0.99) within the range of 0.2 to 20 μg/mL with a detection limit of 0.019 µg/mL. The study indicates quenching of Y3+ was result of dynamic quenching and the Inner Filter Effect (IFE) effect. Also, the cytotoxicity of M-CDs was checked via CAM assay and significant growth in blood vascularization with healthy growth of chick embryo confirmed the M-CDs were biocompatible. The nontoxic M-CDs was employed for MCF-7 breast cancer cell imaging which gives bright blue fluoresce under UV light excitation. Further, aiming to a circular economy approach the residual carbon remaining after hydrothermal was used as reactivated carbon for the abatement of environmental pollutants. The sustainable, cost-effective and agricultural waste-derived Mg-doped CDs have potential applications in analytical, environmental, forensic as well as biomedical fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The dataset generated during and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on the reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Yttrium (Y) is a widely used rare earth element in many electronic devices such as microwave filters, and LEDs in the production of phosphorescence materials which are used in cell phones and large display screens, leasers as well as in radars. However, after discarding these devices; yttrium is eventually contaminates environmental sources such as soil and water which leads to negative effects on aquatic animals and human beings as well as on photosynthesis and plant growth [1]. According to the guidelines of the US EPA and WHO the permissible concentration of yttrium in drinking water should be 6.4 µg/L [2]. Therefore, it is a major concern to maintain contamination of groundwater, rivers, and other water bodies from such hazardous heavy metals like yttrium. Scientist around the world have been developing various methods such as ICP-OES, AAS, ICP-MS, FES, and XRF for the detection of heavy metal ions; however, their expensive instrumentation, delicacy, and high maintenance affect their wide applicability [3–7]. Until now some research groups have developed selective sensing platforms for Y3+ ion in aqueous media by using various sensing techniques. Previously, Salicylaldehyde Acylhydrazone, Cyclen=1,4,7,10-tetraazacyclododecane, CNTs, N-CDs have utilized as Y3+ sensor [8–11]. However, to the best of our knowledge cow dung derived carbon dots were first time used for sensing of Y3+ ions in this report.

Carbon dots (CDs) are one of the members of carbon family shows excellent photoluminescent properties as well as great biocompatibility, water solubility and colloidal as well as photostability [12,13]. These outstanding properties of CDs have been attracting the attention of researchers for various applications such as catalysis, energy storage, anti-counterfeiting, optoelectronics, biomedicine, drug delivery, and sensors, etc [14]. CDs are quasi-spherical shaped materials with amorphous and slightly crystalline carbon core enriched with hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl functional groups and predominant with sp2 carbon. These unique characteristics of CDs lead them towards the fluorescence sensing of various metal ions, hazardous pollutants, in-vivo/ in-vitro imaging and biomedical applications [12,15]. The recent studies indicated that heteroatom/ metal ion doping significantly enhances the optical as well as structural properties of CDs [16]. The transition metal ion doping (Ni, Co, Zn, Gd, Eu, Mg) in CDs have significantly imparts the enhanced tuning of optical properties, surface functionalization, fluorescence lifetime as well as quantum yield (QY) [17–21].

The environmental/ agricultural (natural) waste materials mainly containing lignocellulosic materials, which are highly carbon rich components with considerable amount of oxygen and nitrogen. Therefore, the various natural wastes such as fruit peels, leaves, kitchen wastes, fruit shells, animal-derived, and agricultural wastes are previously used as carbon precursors for carbon dot synthesis [22,23]. Cow dung is the most abundant waste material produced in nature and traditionally used as a biofertilizer containing high amount of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. Barbosa et al., previously used cow dung for the synthesis of carbon dots and effectively applied for fluorescence cell imaging of nucleoli in breast cancer cell lineages (MCF-7) [24]. The synthesis methods have two domains namely top-down and bottom-up approaches. The top-down method gives structural stability with controlled particle size but produces low-yield, and high-cost is a big challenge for researchers [12]. Whereas, the bottom-up methods are relatively cost-effective, environment-friendly, and easy to handle [21,25]. Bottom-up methods like microwave assisted [26,27], pyrolysis [28], and hydrothermal method [27] have been widely used for the synthesis of CDs. The hydrothermal synthesis is quite inexpensive method at low temperature range between 120–300 °C with high quantum yield.

In current research work with the aim to sensing of hazardous transition metal ions from water bodies; we have synthesized magnesium doped carbon dots (M-CDs) from upcycling of agricultural bio-waste cow dung as carbon precursor. A simple and inexpensive one-pot hydrothermal method was employed for the synthesis of M-CDs. The successfully synthesized M-CDs was confirmed by various characterization techniques and applied for sensing of hazardous transition metal ions. The biocompatibility of M-CDs was analyzed by CAM assay. Further, the M-CDs were employed for MCF-7 breast cancer cell imaging. The residue remaining after synthesis was used as it is for the adsorption of environmental pollutants. The agricultural waste upcycling as well as a circular economy approach based M-CDs having potential analytical, sensing, biomedical as well as environmental applications.

Dry cow dung waste collected from agricultural farms near by Kolhapur city. All the chemicals were used of analytical grade. The MgCl2⋅6H2O was used as a Mg dopant. All the metal ion solutions that are used for sensing were prepared using metal salts and biomolecules of analytical grade as follows; NiCl2, CoCl2·6H2O, BaCl2, ZnCl2, HgCl2, SnCl2⋅2H2O, MgCl2⋅6H2O, KMnO4, K2Cr2O7, FeSO4⋅7H2O, CuSO4⋅2H2O, Pb(NO3)2, RuCl3·H2O, CdCl2·H2O, Y(NO3)3·6H2O, KSCN, Na2S, Na2HPO4, Na2SO4, KIO3, KBrO3, KNO3, Na2SO3, L-histidine, Riboflavin, Folic acid, D-tyrosine, Glycine, D-penicillamine, L-tyrosine, N-acetyl L-cysteine, Vit-C, Vit-A, Hemoglobin, K+ and Dopamine were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories (SRL), PVT. LTD. (Mumbai, India). The doubly distilled water (D.W.) was used throughout the experiment.

The fluorescence sensing analysis was carried out at room temperature using PC based spectrofluorometer (Jasco FP 8300, Japan) with 1 cm quartz cuvette. The UV–visible spectrophotometer was used for recording of absorption spectra (Specord 210 plus, Analytikjena, Germany). High-resolution transmittance electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (FEI Tecnai G2, F30) analysis used for understanding of morphology, size and shape analysis of M-CDs. The elemental composition in M-CDs was examined via X-ray Photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using MgKα (1253.6 eV) as an X-ray source over the binding energy range of 0 to 300 eV (JEOL JPS 9030). The X-ray Diffractometer (XRD) Bruker D8 Advance using CuKα radiation for XRD analysis. The surface functionalization of M-CDs was studied by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR Bruker). The fluorescence life time of M-CDs was measured by using Time Correlated Single Photon Count (TCSPC) system Delta DiodeTM Horiba Scientific.

The magnesium doped carbon dots (M-CDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method using cow dung as carbon precursor and MgCl2 as Mg dopant. The dry cow dung was heated in electric furnace for 2 h at 500 °C for achieving carbon black. The 1 g of carbon black was added into 50 mL of distilled water (D.W.) with 0.1 g of MgCl2 in Teflon lined autoclave having 100 mL capacity and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The obtained brown coloured solution was filtered with Whatman filter paper No. 41 and centrifuged for 20 min at 12000 rpm for removal of carbon residue. Further, the M-CDs passed through 0.22 µm syringe filter to avoid large particles and the M-CDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further experiment. The leftover carbon residue further washed with D.W. for several times and dried at 100 °C and stored for further experiment.

The Quantum yield (QY) of M-CDs was calculated using standard comparison method in which quinine sulfate in 0.1 M sulphuric acid used as a reference (0.54 QY at 360 nm, η = 1.33) (Eq. (1)). The absorbance of the reference and sample was kept below 0.1 to avoid self-absorbance effect.
(1)

Q r

Where, Q is quantum yield, Is and Ir are fluorescence intensity of the sample and reference respectively. While, As and Ar are the absorbance of the sample and reference respectively. η is the refractive index of the sample and reference.

The fluorescence sensing of metal ions was carried out by interacting 0.5 mL M-CDs with 0.5 mL (100 ppm) metal ions with 4 mL D.W. to make a 10 ppm concentration of total solution. Sn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Ru3+, Co2+, Fe3+, Y3+, Al3+, Cr6+, Mn7+ metal ions ware used for selectivity experiment. Furthermore, for linearity experiment the concentration of Y3+ varied from 0.2 to 20 µg/mL. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at 310 nm excitation.

The effect of co-existing metal ions was studied by interacting 0.5 mL (100 ppm) of Sn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Ru3+, Co2+, Fe3+, Y3+, Al3+, Cr6+, Mn7+ metal ion solutions with 10 ppm of M-CDs + Y3+ solution and recorder fluorescence spectra at 310 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chick chorioallantoic membrane (CAM) assay for angiogenesis
ce:section.ce:para: The CAM assay was performed for angiogenesis study on fertilized eggs. The eggs were incubated for 5 days at 37 ˚C and 80 % humidity. After incubation eggs were opened on the air sac side, the shell was removed carefully with forceps, and the sample was applied to the CAM which was socked in filter discs. The cavity was then covered with parafilm, and the eggs were further incubated at 37 ˚C with relative humidity of 80 % for the next 11 days. During this period the process of CAM was closely monitored and photographed.

The H and E staining was carried out by placing the tissue sections (on glass slides) in xylene or a xylene substitute to remove the paraffin wax. This typically involves two or three changes of xylene, each for 5–10 min. Transfer the slides through a series of decreasing concentrations of ethanol (100, 95, and 70 %) for 2–5 min each to rehydrate the tissues. Rinse the slides in D.W. for 2–5 min. Immerse the slides in hematoxylin stain for 5–10 min, depending on the specific formulation and desired intensity. Rinse the slides in running tap water for 5–10 min to remove excess hematoxylin. Differentiate the staining by dipping the slides briefly in acid alcohol (optional, if over-staining occurs). Place the slides in ammonia water or lithium carbonate solution for 1–2 min to convert hematoxylin to its blue form. Rinse in running tap water for 5–10 min. Immerse the slides in eosin stain for 1–3 min. Eosin stains the cytoplasm and other eosinophilic (acidic) structures. Rinse briefly in running tap water to remove excess eosin. Transfer the slides through increasing concentrations of ethanol (70, 95, and 100 %) for 1–2 min each to dehydrate the tissue. Clear the slides in xylene or a xylene substitute for 5–10 min. Apply a drop of mounting medium on the tissue section. Cover with a coverslip and remove any air bubbles by gently pressing the coverslip. The slides are now ready for examination under a light microscope.

The cell bio-imaging was studied on MCF-7 cells. The MCF-7 cells were procured from NCCS, Pune and cultured in 10 % FBS, DMEM medium in the presence of 100 mg/L Antibiotics. The cell was cultured in 25 mL flask and kept at 37 ˚C in a humidified atmosphere of 5 % CO2. The subculture was performed in every 2–3 days and media was changed after 48–72 h. The adherent cells were detached from the surface of the cultured flask by trypsinization. The cell was then used for the cell imaging. Further, the cells were seeded in a 12 well microplate and incubate at 37 ˚C in CO2 incubator overnight. The 100 μg/mL of M-CDs was treated with cells for 48 h. After incubation, cells were washed with PBS and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 30 min in addition 20 μL of control also incubated separately for 20 min and finally, fluorescence images of cells were recorded under fluorescence microscope.

The residual carbon was reused after washing for the adsorption of model environmental pollutants. The 10 mg of carbon directly applied without any treatment for adsorption of 10 ppm solution of tetracycline, Paraquat, Chrysoidine, Cr6+, Ru3+ and Mn7+.

The cow dung derived blue emitting M-CDs was highly water soluble. The reaction time and temperature of synthesis of M-CDs was optimized as shown Fig. S1 . The M-CDs shows maximum fluorescence intensity at optimized reaction parameters of 180 ˚C for 8 h. The formation of fluorescent M-CDs was primarily confirmed by different spectroscopic techniques and further characterized by different analytical techniques for better understanding of surface morphology as well as optical properties of M-CDs.

The optical properties of M-CDs were studied by fluorescence and UV–Vis. spectroscopy. The Fig. 1 a. indicates absorbance peak of M-CDs, the characteristic absorbance peak at 263 nm and weak peak at 333 nm are due to π-π* and n-π* transition of sp2 domain of CC and CO functional groups. This proves that carbon nuclei forms conjugated structures during dehydration and condensation reaction [29]. The fluorescence spectrum of M-CDs showing bright blue coloured emission at 420 nm when excited at 310 nm ( Fig. 1a). The excitation wavelength of M-CDs was varied from 300 to 390 as and showing excitation dependent emission. The excitation dependent behaviour of M-CDs was due to difference in particle size leads to different energy levels and the electronic transition states are dependent on phonon energy state of CDs [30]. The quantum yield of M-CDs was measured by the standard comparative method using quinine sulfate as a reference and the QY was found to be 20 %.

The stability of M-CDs was checked under influence of different pH and salt solutions and UV radiation. The pH of the solution was varied from 2 to 12 with addition of 0.5 mL of M-CDs in each pH solution. The Fig. 1c. shows slight enhancement in fluorescence intensity at pH 10 and relatively slight quenching of fluorescence intensity at 2 pH. While, there was no significant changes in other pH solutions. Also, the M-CDs shows good stability in different concentrations of salt solution as shown in Fig. 1 d. The photo-stability of M-CDs was studied by irradiating UV light with the help of 365 nm UV lamp for 0 to 60 min on M-CDs. As Shows in Fig. S2
. fluorescence intensity was quite stable under UV light. Overall, blue emitting M-CDs have good stability in different pH, salt solution and under UV light as well as good quantum yield which is beneficial for further study in fluorescence sensing [31,32].

The surface morphology and particle size of M-CDs was confirmed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique (Fig. 2
). As shown in Fig. 2a–c HR-TEM images with increasing magnification for 10, 5, and 2 nm point out that the M-CDs showing homogeneous distribution of quasi-spherical shaped morphology. The Fig. 2c shows graphitic and slightly crystalline structure of M-CDs with a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm which is highly attributed to (100) plane of graphite. These results indicated that M-CDs has graphene like structure [33].

The faded circular diffraction rings in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern corresponds to (002) and (100) plane in graphitic structure of M-CDs proved the amorphous nature of M-CDs due to distorted graphitic sheets which well matches with XRD results ( Fig. 2d) [34]. The histogram in Fig. 2e indicating that the M-CDs having particle size in the range of 1–6 nm with the average size of 3 nm. Fig. 2f. represents the XRD spectrum of M-CDs, which showing the broad peak at 2θ = 23.3˚ due to (002) plane which attributed to distorted graphitic structure while a small peak 2θ = 43˚ corresponds graphite (100) plane. These results clearly indicating the M-CDs have slightly graphitic distorted nature [35,36]. The outcomes of the TEM, and XRD analysis confirmed the slightly graphitic distorted nature of carbon dots.

The selective detection of metal ions was evaluated for Sn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Ru3+, Co2+, Fe3+, Y3+, Al3+, Cr6+, Mn7+ metal ions. In brief 0.5 mL of metal ion solutions (100 µg/mL) were added separately into different test tubes containing 0.5 mL M-CDs and further diluted with D. W. to achieve 10 µg/mL of final concentration of solution. Further, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at 310 nm excitation. In comparison to other metal ions (Fig. 3 a), a significant fluorescence quenching was observed for the Y3+ metal ion with blue shift of 20 nm. Further, the effect of M-CDs on various anion and biomolecules was also tested. M-CDs was interacted with anions such as SO3 2−, IO3 −, BrO3 −, SCN−, NO3 –, SO4 2−, PO4 2− and S2− as well as biomolecules such as L-histidine, Riboflavin, Folic acid, D-tyrosine, Glycine, D-penicillamine, L-tyrosine, N-acetyl L-cysteine, Vit-C, Vit-A, Hemoglobin, K+, Dopamine as shows in Fig. S3 a and b. Fluorescence intensity of M-CDs does not significantly change in presence of anion and biomolecule. Herein, M-CDs shows selective quenching for Y3+ ion in presence of cations, anions and biomolecules. The F/F0 value of M-CDs in presence of various metal ions shown diminished bar graph selectively for Y3+ (Fig. 3b). The results suggest that M-CDs have excellent selectivity towards Y3+ while, other metal ions have no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of M-CDs.

The sensitivity of the developed probe was investigated using different concentrations of Y3+ varied from 0.2 to 20 µg/mL were added into M-CDs (Fig. 3c). The fluorescence intensity of M-CDs was linearly quenches with increasing concentration of Y3+ with significant blue shift and broadening of peak. This quenching of fluorescence was further studied by using the Stern-Volmer plot in Fig. 3d (Eq. (2)).
(2)

= 1 +

where, F0 and F are fluorescence Intensities of M-CDs in absence and presence of Y3+, Ksv is Stern-Volmer quenching constant and Q is concentration of quencher. The results obtained from Stern-Volmer plot shows good linear range with co-relation coefficient (R2) value of 0.99. The limit of detection for Y3+ was calculated by equation (Eq. (3)) which was founded to be 0.019 µg/mL. Where σ is standard deviation and slope is stern volmer constant k.
(3)
LOD = 3.3 ∗

The Fig. 3e shows the photographic images of selectivity of Y3+ in presence of M-CDs as compared to other metal ions under the influence of UV light. In addition, the Fig. 3f shows the photographic representation of increasing concentration of Y3+ in presence of M-CDs under UV light. The images clearly demonstrated as concentration of Y3+ increases the fluorescence emission of M-CDs decreases gradually. The sensing performance of M-CDs was compared with reported methods for selective sensing of Y3+ ion in Table 1 . As per the best of our knowledge and the literature survey suggests that the M-CDs are the only CDs based fluorescent probe for selective detection of Y3+ ions with low LOD (0.019 μM). Compare to other florescent probe mention below M-CDs was only carbon dots and biocompatible fluorescent probe were used for Y3+ ion sensing with promisingly low limit of detection of 0.019 μM. The selectivity and sensitivity of developed method for detection of Y3+ with respect to other metal ions was explored by co-existing metal ion study (Fig. 4 a). In this study, the aqueous solution of M-CDs was added into Y3+ metal ion solution having concentration of 10 µg/mL and further the competing metal ions were added in the M-CDs + Y3+ solution separately. The fluorescence spectra of the co-existing metal ions were recorded at 310 nm excitation. The bar graph of co-existing metal ions shows that there was no significant effect of the presence of other metal ions on the fluorescence quenching of M-CDs + Y3+ solution. The result concludes that competing metal ions does not interfere on selectivity of Y3+ metal ion.

The fluorescence sensing mechanism was determined by time co-related single photon count study (TCSPC) i.e., fluorescence life time decay profile as well as UV–Vis. and fluorescence spectroscopic studies. The fluorescence quenching behavior was mainly classified into static quenching i.e., ground state complex formation. While, the excited sate interaction between fluorophore and analyte are due to dynamic quenching, FRET, IFE, and PET [41]. As shown in Fig. 4b fluorescence life time of M-CDs in presence and absence of Y3+ was recorded. The life time of M-CDs and M-CDs + Y3+ for concentrations of 2 and 6 µg/mL was 972.13, 636.17 and 556.64 ns respectively, indicating decreasing fluorescence life time resulting energy transfer during the quenching process. The literature report mentioned that decreasing fluorophore’s life time with increasing concentration of quencher leads to dynamic quenching mechanism [42].

The absorbance of M-CDs in presence and absence of Y3+in Fig. 4c shows there was no effect of Y3+ on absorbance of M-CDs. The absorbance peak of M-CDs was completely disappearing in presence of Y3+. On the other hand, absorbance of Y3+ in presence of M-CDs was quite low than that of pristine Y3+, these observations eliminated the possibility of complex formation. Further, in Fig. 4d absorbance spectra of Y3+ with M-CDs overlap with excitation spectra of M-CDs which gives the possibility of Inner Filter Effect (IFE). In literature survey, Fanlin Zu et al. stated that in case of IFE absorption spectrum of quencher was overlapped with excitation or emission spectrum of CDs [41]. In present study, decreasing fluorescence life time of M-CDs in presence of Y3+ with respect to concentration and overlapping of absorbance spectra of Y3+ with excitation spectra of M-CDs resulting a combination of dynamic quenching and IFE mechanism [43].

The XPS spectra in Fig. 5 shows change in XPS spectra of as prepared M-CDs and M-CDs + Y3+. Fig. 5a shows comparative survey spectrum of M-CDs and M-CDs + Y3+, the high resolution XPS spectra confirmed the presence of C 1s, O 1s, Mg 2p, and Mg 2s elements in M-CDs associated with binding energy of 284.7, 531.7, 50.7, and 89.7 eV, respectively [36] while the inset image displays two peak at 157 and 162 eV representing characteristic peak of Y 3d3/2 and Y 3d5/2 respectively of yttrium ion in M-CDs−Y3+ solution [8,44–46]. The Fig. 5b shows the deconvolution of C 1s with various C-binding site at 284.03, 285.00, and 286.40 eV indicated CC, CC, CO bonds present in carbon while in deconvolution spectra of C 1s in Fig. 5 bʹ shifting of peak at 288.9 eV can be clearly seen as well as the intensity of peak at 284 and 286 eV decreases. The result indicated that Y3+ forms strong bonds with oxygen containing groups of M-CDs. The deconvolution of O 1s shown in Fig. 5c describes the CO binding at 532 eV and 533 eV for CO and CO bonds respectively [47]. The comparative deconvolution spectra of O 1s illustrated that reduction of peak intensity at 533.9 eV as shown in Fig. 5 cʹ was validated the strong bond between Y3+ and carboxylic groups from M-CDs. The deconvolution of Mg 2p in Fig. 5d at 50.57 and 51.51 eV is due to MgC and MgO bonds. [48] While, in Fig. 5 dʹ decreasing peak intensity was observed at 51.5 eV, as well as further illustrated that the Mg plays vital role in bond formation. The results indicated that Y3+ ions interacted with oxygen containing groups of M-CDs which leads to energy transfer from M-CDs to Y3+ eventually leads to the florescence quenching. Further, the FTIR spectra before and after addition of Y3+ ion shown in Fig. 5e. The functional groups present on the surface of M-CDs. Wherein, the broad intense peak around 3344 cm−1 indicated the presence of –OH functional groups while, the strong peak at 1639 cm−1 is due to CO starching [49]. The FTIR peak at 2122 and 668 cm−1 is due the stretching vibration of CC bond and COC bond [47]. The peak at ∼582 cm−1 confirmed the presence of the MgO bond [50]. The weakening of absorption peak at 1639.65 cm−1 and change in absorption peaks of FTIR after addition of Y3+ shows bond formation between Y3+ and oxygen containing groups of M-CDs which well matches with XPS data.

The practical application of develop M-CDs system was applied for different environmental water samples. The water samples were collected from Panchaganga river, Shivaji University lake and tap water from Shivaji University. In present study, water samples were collected and filtered through Whatman filter paper No. 41 to remove the residual impurities and boiled for 10 min to remove dissolved gasses. Further, the known amount of Y3+ was spiked into water samples and fluorescence spectra was recorded at 310 nm excitation and results were summarized into Table 2 . The developed M-CDs based fluorescent probe shows good percentage recovery between 94 to 99 % with good relative standard deviation (RSD). This result indicate that the M-CDs can be applied for environmental sample analysis.

CAM assay model is prominently considered for cytotoxicity study and to analyze biocompatibility of material [51,52]. In current study, the eggs were treated with a concentration of 0.4 mg/mL of M-CDs and phosphate buffer saline (PBS) was used as a test control, respectively. The untreated CAM exhibited normal vascular growth with well-developed branching of new blood vessels. However, the CAM treated with M-CDs at the concentration of 0.4 mg/mL showed no disrupted branching or deformation of angiogenesis, as depicted in Fig. 6 a. Angiogenesis is the formation of new blood vessels from pre-exiting once. It’s crucial for organ development, wound healing, and embryonic growth. However, it’s also exploited by disease like cancer to fuel tumor growth. The main advantage in using the CAM assay is the potential to collect information with regards to biocompatibility, integration and efficacy (angiogenic response, tissue regeneration/ formation) of biomaterials that is M-CDs. The sample from CAM area was selected for the histological study and stained with H&E staining as shown in Fig. 6b. The central mesenchymal layer contain nuclei stained viable cell with purple colure which shows non-toxic nature of M-CDs [53,54].

The properties like high fluorescence intensity, robust stability, and favorable biocompatibility, makes the M-CDs to be utilized for biological and biomedical applications. As a proof of concept, the aqueous solution of M-CDs was used for cancer cell imaging in this study. The MCF-7 cells were selected as a model for cellular imaging. As indicated in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence microscopic images demonstrate that M-CDs can readily enter into cells of MCF-7. When the cells were incubated with M-CDs for 48 h, bright fluorescence was observed (Fig. 7). MCF-7 cells can bind and uptake the M-CDs on the surface in a short time. As shown in behind the M-CDs, DAPI selected as control and these MCF-7 cells staining using DAPI being incubated with M-CDs for 1 h, MCF-7 cells show much lower fluorescence intensity than M-CDs. Thus M-CDs enable to interacted with cancer cell, and brighten the fluorescence signal was visualized in M-CDs treated cells [55,56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of model environmental pollutants by activated carbon
ce:section.ce:para:
The circular economy and waste valorization approach was employed and the residual carbon after reaction was collected and further used as its as activated carbon for adsorption of hazardous environmental pollutants (Tetracycline (TC), Chrysoidine (CS), Paraquat (PQ), Cr6+, Ru3+ and Mn7+). In this study, 10 mg of activated carbon was added into 10 mL of 10 mg/L solution of each model pollutant. The activated carbon effective for adsorption of above model pollutants (Fig. 8
). The Table 3 . represents the removal efficiency (%) of pollutants which was calculated by using equation (4) and (5) respectively.
(4)

where, Qe (mg/L) is adsorption efficiency, C0 and Ce (mg/L) is initial and equilibrium concentration of pollutants, V (L) is volume of solution and m (mg) is the mass of the activated carbon.

In the present study, sustainable agricultural waste valorization approach was applied for synthesis of fluorescent M-CDs from cow dung. The optical and physiochemical properties of M-CDs was thoroughly analyzed by various characterization techniques. The M-CDs shows bright blue emission under UV range having excitation wavelength of 310 nm while, emission at 420 nm with 20 % quantum yield. The M-CDs were relatively stable in diverse pH solutions, salt solution of different concentrations and also showed good photostability. Further, the M-CDs was selectively and sensitively employed for detection of Y3+ metal ions by fluorescence spectroscopy method. The limit of detection of developed probe for Y3+ ions was founded to be 0.019 µg/mL. Further, the interaction between Y3+ and M-CDs was investigated by TCSPC, UV–Vis. and fluorescence spectroscopy, XPS and FTIR analysis. The quenching mechanism suggesting the energy transfer takes place from M-CDs to Y3+ ion due to the interaction between oxygen containing groups of M-CDs and Y3+ in excited state. Herein, the angiogenesis of M-CDs on fertilized egg embryo illustrated biocompatibility of M-CDs. The nontoxic as well as biocompatible M-CDs shows bright blue fluorescence when treated with MCF-7 breast cancer cell under UV light excitation compare to DAPI stain. The leftover carbon from synthesis procedure was utilized for remediation of water from various model environmental pollutants. The overall results concluded that the M-CDs have potential application in fluorescence sensing, cancer cell imaging and can be potentially utilized for environmental remediation.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sneha V. Koparde is currently working as a Ph.D. research scholar at the Fluorescence Spectroscopy Research Laboratory (FSRL), Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur, MS, India. and awarded with MAHAJYOTI Research Fellowship. Her major research interest in the synthesis of doped fluorescent carbon dots for sensing and detection of hazardous pollutants as well as environmental remediation.

Omkar S. Nille recently awarded his Ph.D. from the Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur, MS, India. His research interest concerns about synthesis of carbon-based nanomaterials and their composite hydrogels for various applications in the field of sensing, environmental remediation and biomedical applications. He has published research papers, review articles and book chapters in various reputed journals.

Akanksha G. Kolekar have completed her Master’s Degree and recently awarded Ph.D. degree from the Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur, MS, India. Her major research interest is synthesis of doped fluorescent nanomaterials. She has published her research work in a reputed international journal.

Pranoti A. Kamble is currently working as a Ph.D. research scholar at the Centre for Interdisciplinary Research, Department of Stem cell and Regenerative medicine and Medical Biotechnology,D.Y.Patil Education Society (Deemed to be University), Kolhapur, MS, India. Her research interest concerns about preparation of biofunctionalized carbon quantum dots for cancer detection and biomedical applications.

Arpita Pandey Tiwari is currently working as anAssociate Professor, Department of Stem Cell and Regenerative Medicine and Medical Biotechnology, Centre for Interdisciplinary Research, D.Y.Patil Education Society (Deemed to be University), Kolhapur. Her research interest focused on synthesis and development of biomaterials,metal nanoparticles, and fluorescent carbon nanomaterials for multifaceted biomedical applications and published outcomes in several reputed international journals.

Govind B. Kolekar is currently serving as a Senior Professor of physical chemistry in the Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur, MS, India. He has guided several Ph.D. research scholars with expertise in the field of fluorescence sensing, decontamination of hazardous pollutants as well as biomedical applications. Also, he has awarded with various prestigious awards. He has published several research papers in well-known international journals as well as worked as a reviewer of many reputed international journals.

Samadhan P. Pawar is currently working as an Assistant Professor in the Department of Chemistry, Rajarshi Chhatrapati Shahu College, Kolhapur, MS, India. Currently 4 Ph.D. scholars working under his guidance. His research expertise in the synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials for sensing applications and environmental remediation by adsorption phenomena. He has published research papers in renowned international journals and working as reviewer for many national and international journals.
